issue_title: optimize the sending results from worker nodes for data frames
issue_body:
currently, the package [concurrently](https://github.com/alphatwirl/alphatwirl/tree/a106f1f8229ed5d6ac9a24e8198e8bafe0406891/alphatwirl/concurrently), uses pickle to send the results from the worker nodes.

pickle files with data frames tend to become large. and loading large pickles is slow.

develop an alternative implementation which is specifically optimized for data frames, for example, by using [dask](http://dask.pydata.org/en/latest/) so that loading the results at the interactive node fast.

issue_title: Difficult to customize basket message logic (offer gained, offer lost, new total)
issue_body:
`apps.basket.views.get_messages` and `apps.basket.views.apply_messages` aren't customizable. It's possible to change the messages by overriding the templates, but if you want to pass in some additional context or disable a message completely, there's no good way to do that.

One simple solution would be to move `get_messages` and `apply_messages` to a class and then load in the class with `get_class`.

issue_title: Why do we need uk and uk_UA?
issue_body:
Hello!

Could you, please, explain to me why there are two files for Ukrainian translations: `uk` and `uk_UA`. And the main issue is that there are not all translations in the `uk_UA` file: https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/locale/uk_UA/LC_MESSAGES/django.po#L4763

There is not even `uk_UA` language in django settings: https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/conf/global_settings.py#L135

So why do we need uk_UA in django-oscar? And, why django chose it, but not `uk` file?

Also, there are files for Russian: `ru` and `ru_RU`. And `ru` is not translated completely but `ru_RU` is.

issue_title: Inconsistent model instance creation in tests
issue_body:
Looking through the test suite, there are fully 3 different ways of creating `Product` instances. Which way is considered the "most correct" way?

* In `tests.functional.dashboard.catalogue_tests`, `Product` is imported using `get_model` and manipulated directly. Why doesn't it use a factory? If there's a reason for it, there should be a comment in the code.
* In `tests.functional.catalogue.catalogue_tests`, some tests use `oscar.test.factories.create_product`, and other tests use `oscar.test.factories.ProductFactory`.
* In `tests.functional.dashboard.product_tests`, some tests use `ProductFactory` and other tests use `Product` directly, but this time imported using `from oscar.apps.catalogue.models import Product`

Perhaps also confusingly, `oscar.test.factories.create_stockrecord` will implicitly create a product if you don't pass one in.

I think we should pick one way and consistently use it throughout the test suite. *I have already set aside the time to fix this issue, I just need feedback on which way I need to go.*

issue_title: Use keepalive
issue_body:
Using keepalive means that the TCP and SSL sessions do not have to be restarted for each request. This significantly reduces latency, particularly from locations such as Australia (200ms ping time to graph.facebook.com)

This can easily be achieved by using requests.Session

issue_body:
I suspect an issue in request()

        if self.access_token:
            # If post_args exists, we assume that args either does not exists
            # or it does not need `access_token`.

args["access_token"] = self.access_token

        graph.access_token = page_token

issue_title: Drop support for EOL Python versions?
issue_body:
I'd like to suggest pyflakes only supports non-EOL Python versions.

Python 2.5, 2.6, 3.2 and 3.3 are EOL and no longer receive security updates.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1324225/33826009-275286e4-de6c-11e7-8f70-781433006423.png)

https://github.com/PyCQA/pyflakes#pyflakes says:

> It is available on PyPI and it supports all active versions of Python from 2.5 to 3.6.

It can be argued that 2.5-2.6 and 3.0-3.3 are no longer active and no longer need support.

Here's the pip installs for pyflakes from PyPI for the last month (via `pypinfo --percent --pip --markdown pyflakes pyversion`) showing low or non-existent numbers for the EOL versions.

| python_version | percent | download_count |
| -------------- | ------: | -------------: |
| 2.7            |   54.1% |        582,452 |
| 3.6            |   20.5% |        221,023 |
| 3.5            |   17.2% |        185,122 |
| 3.4            |    6.8% |         72,930 |
| 3.3            |    0.6% |          6,432 |
| 2.6            |    0.4% |          4,789 |
| 3.7            |    0.4% |          4,226 |
| 3.2            |    0.0% |            414 |
| None           |    0.0% |              4 |

Some other PyCQA projects are planning on ending all Python 2 support around mid-2018 when Python 3.7 is due out (https://github.com/PyCQA/meta/issues/19).

issue_title: algolia_reindex command resets the ranking formula.
issue_body: We use Algolia with Django and once a day we reindex our data. We need to reindex it even with enabled auto-indexing. Unfortunately, algolia_reindex command resets our ranking formula.

issue_title: identical "time" values in ibmqx3 job execution results don't seem correct
issue_body:
When looking at job-results I noticed that the job's executions on ibmqx3 always have identical "time" values (down to the 14th digit) even when their QASM programs differed in length and complexity.
(Simulations give different "time" values even on the millisecond scale (assuming that the time unit is milliseconds) and even for identical QASM programs per execution.)

Is the "time" value set to the same value for all ibmqx3 executions of a single job or is that a mistake?
Could it be that the value in "time" is the cumulated time taken for all executions of this job together?  
At least the "date" fields varies for executions of a job on ibmqx3, on the scale of seconds even.


For clarification the "time" and "date" fields I'm referring to are:

{"qasms":[{"qasm":"include \"qelib1.inc\";qreg q[16] ...",
           "status":"DONE",
           "executionId":"...",
           "result":{**"date"**:"...",
           "data":{

           "counts": ...

*Scenario*
Clients wants to increase productivity by defining their own templates and exposing them using the usual Pulumi CLI experience.

*Use case*

*Considerations*

* To setup a repository as a template there should be an interface to flag a github repository (plus probably a pulumi template manifest), this will delegate team approval and permissions to what git usage already provides.

POST /v1/apps/example-go/domains/ HTTP/1.1

{'domain': 'example.example.com', 'app': 'example-go'}
~~~

issue_title: Don't build all targets of all subprojects by default
issue_body:
A general question: **how can we control naturally and easily which targets are built, in a natural way?**

When using subprojects, it would make sense to not build all targets by default, but only those which are depended on by any target of the root project. Probably, it should be the command line which allows one to build **all** targets?

issue_title: sync uses acquired field, should use new published field
Model prediction should fail if it finds no known features
issue_body:
The following case should be an error: train a model on some data then call `predict` with completely unrelated data (i.e. no features in common with the training set). 

All of turicreate's regression and classification models produce predictions without errors. The following code works without any errors or warnings, and each model gives a prediction:
```python
import turicreate as tc


def check_all_missing_column_classifier_predict(create_method):
    data = tc.SFrame({'feature1': [9,9,1,1], 'feature2': [1,1,9,9], 'label': ['a','a','b','b']})
    model = tc.boosted_trees_classifier.create(data, target='label')

    other_data = tc.SFrame({'feature3': [5]})
    result = model.predict(other_data)
    print("Prediction: {}".format(result))


check_all_missing_column_classifier_predict(tc.boosted_trees_classifier)
check_all_missing_column_classifier_predict(tc.decision_tree_classifier)
check_all_missing_column_classifier_predict(tc.logistic_classifier)
check_all_missing_column_classifier_predict(tc.nearest_neighbor_classifier)
check_all_missing_column_classifier_predict(tc.random_forest_classifier)
check_all_missing_column_classifier_predict(tc.svm_classifier)


def check_all_missing_column_regressor_predict(create_method):
    data = tc.SFrame({'feature1': [9,9,1,1], 'feature2': [1,1,9,9], 'y': [0,0,10,10]})
    model = tc.boosted_trees_classifier.create(data, target='y')

    other_data = tc.SFrame({'feature3': [5]})
    result = model.predict(other_data)
    print("Prediction: {}".format(result))

check_all_missing_column_regressor_predict(tc.boosted_trees_regression)
check_all_missing_column_regressor_predict(tc.decision_tree_regression)
check_all_missing_column_regressor_predict(tc.linear_regression)
check_all_missing_column_regressor_predict(tc.random_forest_regression)
```

issue_title: Return a numerical value based on years for sorting
issue_body:
The current `lower_` and `upper_` methods return `date` objects, and are thus limited to `date.min` and `date.max`. This is limiting when it comes to long years, and means that all dates before 0 sort at the same amount.

It's probably better to define a method to return an integer form of EDTF lower and upper bounds to use for sorting.

Where only year is available, return a (long) integer. Where month and day are available, return a float according to the fraction of the year.

issue_title: Girder-friendly locks
issue_body:
I'm currently working on a GW task in which I download data from a Girder item, update that data, and then upload back to the item. Obviously, if more than one instance of this task is run at the same time, the update from one could overwrite the other, so I would like to use a lock to assert ownership over the item while the task does its work.

Celery has documentation for how to do this with the Django cache [here](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/tutorials/task-cookbook.html). I'm wondering how best to do the equivalent of this in Girder. This may be a matter of developing a Girder plugin with endpoints to facilitate lock acquisition, or there may be some lighter solution which could be added to the documentation. I'm fairly agnostic on exactly what form the solution might take, but I'm interested in what other people think would be the best way to handle it.

issue_title: LANGUAGE variable in Makefile elided by LANGUAGE environment variable
issue_body:
The LANGUAGE variable in the Makefile is elided by the LANGUAGE environment variable. 

On many systems (including Ubuntu and MSYS2), the LANGUAGE environment variable is defined, e.g. as "en_US" or something like that.

This leads to `make` producing output like this:

```
$ make
mkdir -p ./gens
protoc --proto_path=.:/usr/local/include --en_US_out=./gens --grpc_out=./gens --plugin=protoc-gen-grpc=/usr/local/bin/grpc_en_US_plugin google/api/annotations.proto
protoc-gen-en_US: program not found or is not executable
--en_US_out: protoc-gen-en_US: Plugin failed with status code 1.
Makefile:41: recipe for target 'google/api/annotations.pb.cc' failed
make: *** [google/api/annotations.pb.cc] Error 1
```

Changing the variable name in the Makefile to something different - anything not already defined in the environment - fixes the issue.

issue_title: BigQuery: document proper use of run_async_query/QueryJob/QueryResult
issue_body:
If I run an async query using `run_async_query()`, get a `QueryJob` from it and a `QueryResult` from that by calling `job.query_results()`, the `rows` attribute of QR is empty. It seems that when the `GET` request is sent a parameter `maxResults=0` is automatically added, in the client code, which explains why rows is empty.

But this is confusing and not how it behaves for sync queries. I think it should allow getting some rows in that first `GET` request.

Can you please document the proper use of async queries and QueryResult? Thank you.

issue_title: feature request: better api for arbitrary keyword argument pass to query ?
issue_body:
from this example https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/bigquery-usage.html#querying-data-asynchronous

```python
>>> dataset = client.dataset('dataset_name')
>>> table = dataset.table(name='person_ages')
>>> job = client.run_async_query('fullname-age-query-job', query)
>>> job.destination = table
>>> job.write_disposition= 'WRITE_TRUNCATE'
>>> job.name
'fullname-age-query-job'
>>> job.job_type
'query'
>>> job.created
None
>>> job.state
None
```

how about a better api by allowing arbitrary keyword list to be appended on the insert job POST request
https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/jobs the keyword `destinationTable` and `writeDisposition` here are from rest api reference, this way can keep `google-cloud-python` library to be a thinner layer, just gives user the best flexibility

```python
>>> job = client.run_async_query(query,
          destinationTable={'datasetId': 'dataset_name', 'tableId': 'person_ages'},
          writeDisposition='WRITE_TRUNCATE',
          )
```

in python it can be implemented as dict as last argument:

```python
def run_async_query(query, **kwargs):
    # populate from kwargs to be on the `configuration.query` in the post request body,
    # it could optionally have jobId
```

Btw, from #3209 I am not puting a `job_name` / `jobid` field because I don't think that's necessary, the `run_async_query` shouldn't require user to provide a `jobid`, in the rest api insert job request, if jobid is absent, server side would generate a unique id

issue_title: High latency
issue_body:
[b/8334662](http://b/8334662)

I'm consistently seeing hight latency numbers when using the GCD - about 1.5 seconds on average for queries (10 items or less) and single item writes ( < 1kb). 

My ping time to the API endpoint is about 50ms, so discounting a 100ms round trip, that still leaves more than a second to GCD latency. This simply won't work for a server environment, certainly not one that scales. This is very surprising because I was expecting latencies much closer to GAE: https://code.google.com/status/appengine/detail/hr-datastore/2013/06/05#ae-trust-detail-hr-datastore-query-latency

Can we get a dashboard like the GAE HR Datastore status board? Possibly measuring latencies from Google Compute Engine instances and a few different AWS Regions?

issue_title: Read-only cells not really read-only
issue_body:
It seems that read-only cells can still be edited by students. "Read-only" simply means that any changes the students make to the cells will be overwritten by the original cell. 

Since students are given no indication that a cell is read-only, this has the potential to cause lots of confusion. I had to regrade about a quarter of the assignments in my class, since they included critical imports or variable definitions in a "read-only" cell. The students were able to run their own notebooks just fine, but I ran into errors when I ran theirs, since I only had the original "read-only" cell without the imports.

If the cells are called "read-only", then it seems that the cells should be locked for editing. I would suggest eliminating the feature otherwise, since it causes confusion for new instructors.

issue_title: Fix rendering of single quotes in pdfTeX output
issue_body:
Currently, when saving a notebook to PDF via LaTeX, any single quotes in notebook are rendered as unicode apostrophes.
- before: '
- after: ’  ([U+2019](http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/2019/index.htm)?)

This has a couple of problematic side-effects, particularly when rendering Python, etc. code output:
1. Code cannot be copied and pasted into a console
2. Scripts cannot be executed without specifying a unicode coding, even when the only apostrophes are in comment sections.

It seems like there should be an option at the pdfTeX level to specify that ascii characters should be used in place of the apostrophes (e.g., http://tex.stackexchange.com/questions/63345/how-to-make-a-real-apostrophe-or-single-quote-in-latex/63348#63348)

It would be great if this could be implemented in Jupyter's PDF generation.

See also: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/32973952/ipython-notebook-single-quotes-rendered-incorrectly-in-pdf-output
## Version info
- Arch Linux (4.2.1 64-bit)
- Python 2.7.10 / 3.5.0
- IPython 4.0.0
- pdfTeX, Version 3.14159265-2.6-1.40.16

issue_title: Equal resource objects don't necessarily have the same hash
issue_body:
For example,

```
>>> x = ec2.InternetGateway('igw-e98b518c')
>>> y = ec2.InternetGateway('igw-e98b518c')
>>> x == y
True
>>> hash(x) == hash(y)
False
```

This messes up all sorts of assumptions that python likes to make about `hashable` objects, for example when using them in a `set`, and leads to inconsistent results.

issue_title: boto3 should provide a way to obtain AWS RequestIds

AFAICT looking at the code, the only place that `boto3` reports the request ID is [when the request as a whole gets logged](https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/cb6a49a7f7ca6f81f8adb047a278f5f332e95954/boto3/resources/action.py#L154); this is _really_ hard to work with, since the response body is _huge_.

If the request ID were passed as extra data to the logging call, I could extract it via a custom handler. Or some sort of callback system, etc.

issue_title: botocore 1.9.x doesn't raise ExpiredToken error
issue_body:
s3client.Object's `upload_file` method does no longer raise `ExpiredToken` error if the given token expires in botocore 1.9.x. I have this code and catch `ClientError` such that the upper stream can renew the token. This works fine before 1.9.0:
```
try
...
            akey = meta[u'client'].Object(s3location.bucket_name, s3path)
            akey.upload_file(
                data_file,
                Callback=meta[u'put_callback'](
                    data_file,
                    os.path.getsize(data_file),
                    output_stream=meta[u'put_callback_output_stream']) if
                        meta[u'put_callback'] else None,
                ExtraArgs={
                    u'Metadata': s3_metadata,
                    u'ContentEncoding': u'gzip',
                },
                Config=TransferConfig(
                    multipart_threshold=SnowflakeS3Util.DATA_SIZE_THRESHOLD,
                    max_concurrency=max_concurrency,
                    num_download_attempts=10,
                )
            )
...
        except botocore.exceptions.ClientError as err:
            if err.response[u'Error'][u'Code'] == 'ExpiredToken':
                logger.debug(u"AWS Token expired. Renew and retry")
                meta[u'result_status'] = ResultStatus.RENEW_TOKEN
                return
```
But the new botocore 1.9.0+ returns HTTP 400 but doesn't raise `ExpiredToken` error code.

Is there any way to detect the token expiration?

issue_title: pip-accel is not compatible with `-e` editable installs of packages that use `setuptools_scm`
issue_body:
`setuptools_scm` is "the blessed package to manage your versions by scm tags". So your package version will be determined by a git tag, instead of a hard coded string inside your application code. So you don't need a "bump version" commit for every source code change just to release a new package.

`pip-accel` downloads `-e` editable packages from git to a bare repository in a temporary directory, and then checks out a working copy from the bare repository into a `src` directory, then tries to install.

Problem is, this working copy does not have access to the git repository from which it was checked out. So `setuptools_scm` is unable to determine its version number, and installation fails.

`setuptools_scm` is designed to allow installation without access to the git repository only when it is already packaged and this metadata is baked into the egg. Without this metadata, and without access to the git repository, `setuptools_scm` cannot work from a bare working copy.

`pip-accel` should behave more like pip, and clone a normal repository to the `src` directory and install from there.

Output(t:Z, c:Z, c:Z) printtuples

Input("a","active",1).
```
There should be only one tuple dervied for relation `invent_1` and `Output`.

issue_title: (get-value) in incremental UFBV returns variable in term that can be a ground term
issue_body:
Z3 v4.6.0 32-bit on Windows 7

Running this script through the command line...

    (set-option :produce-models true)
    (set-logic UFBV)
    (declare-fun n1 ((_ BitVec 1)) (_ BitVec 32))
    (declare-fun n5 ((_ BitVec 1)) (_ BitVec 32))
    (assert (forall ((i1 (_ BitVec 1))) (= (n5 i1) (ite (= i1 #b0) (n1 i1) #x00000002))))
    (assert (forall ((i1 (_ BitVec 1))) (= (n1 i1) #x00000001)))
    (push 1)
    (check-sat)
    (get-value ((n5 #b0) (n5 #b1)))

...gives the following output:

    (((n5 #b0) (n1 (:var 0)))
     ((n5 #b1) #x00000002))

My client program is choking trying to parse the `(n1 (:var 0))` because it is not a bit vector literal. The only satisfying assignment for `(n5 #b0)` is `#x00000001`. While it is true that `(n1 i1)` is equal to `#x00000001` for any `i1`, shouldn't Z3 be outputting a variable-free ground term here? The SMT-LIB spec says the results of `(get-value)` are value terms of the relevant logic, and I'm asking to evaluate single bit vector-sorted expressions, not entire arrays or functions.

I have only seen this occur in incremental mode so far. Commenting out the `(push 1)` line makes Z3 output a bit vector literal as usual.

issue_title: Quantifier no longer instantiated if conjunct is added
issue_body:
The following program is expected to be yield `unsat`, and does. However, uncommenting the additional conjunct in the quantifier AX-1 changes the result to `unknown`.

```
(set-option :auto_config false)
(set-option :smt.mbqi false)

(declare-fun foo (Int) Bool)
(declare-const k Real)
(declare-fun p (Int) Bool)

(assert (forall ((i Int)) (!
  (and
    (< 0.0 k)
;    (p i) ;;; ---- uncomment this line ----
  )
  :pattern ((foo i))
  :qid |AX-1|)))

(assert (forall ((i Int)) (!
  (foo i)
  :pattern ((foo i))
  :qid |AX-2|)))

(declare-const j Int)
(assert (< j 0))

; (push) ;;; doesn't make a difference
(assert (not
  (ite
    (foo j)
    (< 0.0 k)
    false)))

; (set-option :smt.qi.profile true)
(check-sat)
; (get-info :all-statistics)
; (pop)
```

The quantifier instantiation statistics show that `AX-2` is instantiated in both cases, but `AX-1` is only instantiated if the additional conjunct is not included. My assumption is that in the latter case, Z3 eliminates the quantifier since the quantified variable doesn't occur in the body.

However, I find it surprising that the version with the additional conjunct - in which Z3 presumably doesn't eliminate the quantifier - yields unknown, since the trigger for the quantifier (`(foo j)`) should be available.

issue_title: modules are too long and have too many symbols
issue_body:
Several modules have become quite long which IMO makes them difficult to use and maintain. Obviously modules can be any size and structure, but I think that long files are difficult to navigate, and modules with too many symbols are error prone due to shadowing and namespace collision.

| module | number of lines | number of symbols |
| --- | --: | --: |
| `pvlib.pvsystem` | 2211 | 23 |
| `pvlib.clearsky` | 489 | 13 |
| `pvlib.atmosphere` | 472 | 6 |
| `pvlib.forecast` | 1113 | 7 |
| `pvlib.irradiance` | 1949 | 23 |
| `pvlib.location` | 287 | 1 |
| `pvlib.modelchain` | 722 | 3 |
| `pvlib.solarposition` | 840 | 12 |
| `pvlib.spa` | 1300 | 50 |
| `pvlib.tmy` | 497 | 8 |
| `pvlib.tools` | 259 | 14 |
| `pvlib.tracking` | 523 | 3 |

In particular [`pvsystem`](../blob/master/pvlib/pvsystem.py), [`forecast`](../blob/master/pvlib/forecast.py), [`irradiance`](../blob/master/pvlib/irradiance.py) and [`spa`](../blob/master/pvlib/spa.py) are very long and contain a lot of symbols.

I don't know what would be ideal, but IMO I think a maximum of 500 lines and 10 symbols is a good baseline.

The SPA algorithm presents a unique challenge, as this code was ported, so maybe for reference sake, it should stay as it is. However, I think that the very large arrays of coefficients in `irradiance` and `spa` take a lot of space. These could easily be moved to HDF5 files using [`h5py`](http://www.h5py.org/) which would have some memory and speed enhancements, but I'll create a related issue for that.
# References for large modules and many symbols.
1. [Programmers-SE: Are there any negative side effects of splitting up large modules? [closed]](http://programmers.stackexchange.com/a/139875/46372)
2. [Wikipedia: God Object](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/God_object)
3. [Wikipedia: Anti-Pattern](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anti-pattern)

issue_title: slow performance when creating empty DataFrame in ``Location.get_airmass``
issue_labels: [{'id': 286475252, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyODY0NzUyNTI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/easy', 'name': 'easy', 'color': '006b75', 'default': False}, {'id': 961364188, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjEzNjQxODg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': '97f4ee', 'default': True}, {'id': 961364003, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjEzNjQwMDM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '3bcc79', 'default': True}, {'id': 987081529, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODcwODE1Mjk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/performance', 'name': 'performance', 'color': 'e06411', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Creating an empty DataFrame (no data, no index) and then filling it key by key can cause performance issues in some situations. I believe the issue is due to the way that pandas computes joins on the index. This happens in two places in pvlib: ``Location.get_airmass`` and ``ModelChain.prepare_inputs``. (determined with ``grep -r 'pd.DataFrame()' pvlib``). 

``Location.get_airmass``: this is most relevant with shorter input lengths, especially if ``solar_position`` is not supplied. I discovered this bottleneck when profiling a loop that called ``ModelChain.run_model`` on daily weather data.

``ModelChain.prepare_inputs``: this only an issue there if the user does not supply any weather data, in which case clear sky calculations will be run and the results assigned to the empty DataFrame. Less likely that anyone is running into a significant performance issue here due to the additional calculations, including a linke turbidity lookup.

Here's the key part of ``Location.get_airmass`` using an input of 1440 times, followed by two alternative implementations:

```python
%%timeit
airmass_relative = pvlib.atmosphere.relativeairmass(solar_position['zenith'])
pressure = pvlib.atmosphere.alt2pres(altitude)
airmass_absolute = pvlib.atmosphere.absoluteairmass(airmass_relative, pressure)

airmass = pd.DataFrame()
airmass['airmass_relative'] = airmass_relative
airmass['airmass_absolute'] = airmass_absolute
23.9 ms ± 780 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)
```

Alternative 1:

```python
%%timeit
airmass_relative = pvlib.atmosphere.relativeairmass(solar_position['zenith'])
pressure = pvlib.atmosphere.alt2pres(altitude)
airmass_absolute = pvlib.atmosphere.absoluteairmass(airmass_relative, pressure)

airmass = pd.DataFrame(index=solar_position.index)
airmass['airmass_relative'] = airmass_relative
airmass['airmass_absolute'] = airmass_absolute
1.69 ms ± 28.4 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
```

Alternative 2:

```python
%%timeit
airmass_relative = pvlib.atmosphere.relativeairmass(solar_position['zenith'])
pressure = pvlib.atmosphere.alt2pres(altitude)
airmass_absolute = pvlib.atmosphere.absoluteairmass(airmass_relative, pressure)

airmass = pd.DataFrame({'airmass_relative': airmass_relative, 'airmass_absolute': airmass_absolute})
airmass = airmass[['airmass_relative', 'airmass_absolute']]  # adds 0.4 ms, but guarantees same output 
1.43 ms ± 40.1 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
```

Either 1 or 2 could work for ``Location.get_airmass``. Only 1 would easily work for ``ModelChain.run_model``.

**Versions:**
 - ``pvlib.__version__``: '0.5.2+16.g58f95e0'
 - ``pandas.__version__``: '0.23.1'
 - python: 3.6.5

Approximately reproduced on python 3.5 and pandas 0.17.

line profiler:
<details>

```
   258         1          2.0      2.0      0.0          if solar_position is None:
   259                                                       solar_position = self.get_solarposition(times)
   260                                           
   261         1          3.0      3.0      0.0          if model in atmosphere.APPARENT_ZENITH_MODELS:
   262         1         44.0     44.0      0.1              zenith = solar_position['apparent_zenith']
   263                                                   elif model in atmosphere.TRUE_ZENITH_MODELS:
   264                                                       zenith = solar_position['zenith']
   265                                                   else:
   266                                                       raise ValueError('{} is not a valid airmass model'.format(model))
   267                                           
   268         1       1466.0   1466.0      3.6          airmass_relative = atmosphere.relativeairmass(zenith, model)
   269                                           
   270         1          9.0      9.0      0.0          pressure = atmosphere.alt2pres(self.altitude)
   271         1          1.0      1.0      0.0          airmass_absolute = atmosphere.absoluteairmass(airmass_relative,
   272         1        639.0    639.0      1.6                                                        pressure)
   273

issue_title: Passing (n x 1) numpy arrays as vectors in Eigen
Feature request for exception translators
issue_body:
Pybind11 nicely converts exceptions derived from `std::exception` into corresponding Python exceptions. However it does not currently seem to support custom exceptions. In Boost Python this functionality is provided by exception translators (http://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_61_0/libs/python/doc/html/reference/high_level_components/boost_python_exception_translato.html).
What are your thoughts about adding equivalent functionality to pybind11?

The following may be more appropriate:
“az component and subcommands are not available with the current Azure CLI installation.
If installed with apt-get, please use apt-get to update this installation.”

issue_title: Azure CLI should support multi-delete of resources from own queries
issue_body:
Using the CLI on a mac, installed through `brew`

When I run:

```
az resource delete --ids $(az resource list -g aci-builders --query "[].id" --resource-type "Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups")
```

I get:

```
az resource: error: argument --ids: invalid ResourceId value: '['
```

Which makes sense, given the output of `az resource list -g aci-builders --query "[].id" --resource-type "Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups"`, however as a user, this doesn't make sense at all - why can't the `--ids` parameter also accept a JSON array of IDs instead of a space-separated string?

issue_title: [DNS] Remove '--location' parameter from 'az network dns zone create'
issue_body: The 'location' for DNS zone resources is **always** "global". It should not be exposed as a parameter on `az network dns zone create`.

```

When I type `az login --username <my_username> --password <my_password>`, I get the following error:

```
 az login -u jswails@MYDOMAIN.com
Password:
Get Token request returned http error: 400 and server response: {"error":"invalid_grant","error_description":"AADSTS70002: Error validating credentials. AADSTS50008: SAML token is invalid. AADSTS50006: The element with ID '_877ed6b2-409b-45a9-9cce-216b61d460b3' was either unsigned or the signature was invalid.\r\nTrace ID: edabfd9e-2b6e-41cf-87c0-b3d59ce90000\r\nCorrelation ID: 43366876-8bb2-4bb5-a76a-93a77f7ce525\r\nTimestamp: 2018-05-03 20:13:42Z","error_codes":[70002,50008,50006],"timestamp":"2018-05-03 20:13:42Z","trace_id":"edabfd9e-2b6e-41cf-87c0-b3d59ce90000","correlation_id":"43366876-8bb2-4bb5-a76a-93a77f7ce525"}
```

Some background information:

* This command used to work.  It stopped working right around the time our organization made a change to our SSO applications
* This command *still* works on machines in the organization that have never installed/used `az` before (i.e., if I install az and run this command, it works).  But it fails for every machine that has already installed and used "az" before our SSO settings updates (for every account that tries)
* Rebooting the computer does nothing
* Wiping the ~/.azure directory does nothing
* Uninstalling and reinstalling the Azure CLI does nothing

It looks like when our SSO settings were updated, there was a cert change that's not being picked up by my machine, and the new private cert on our login server isn't able to verify the signature on the SAML token signed by my machine.

So I guess my question is: where can I find this certificate to delete it??  I have been staring at this forever, but am at a complete loss.  The powershell cmdlets for logging into Azure continue to work, but az does not.

issue_title: Need a better way to test for errors
issue_body:
With VCRTestBase we had the ability to allow commands to succeed if an expected error was encountered.

The current scenario test allows for "expect_failure" but it will allow the command to succeed if ANY error is encountered.

The mechanism of using:
```Python
with self.assertRaises(CLIError):
   self.cmd(...)
```

Doesn't work consistently and is requiring changing CLIError to AssertRaises. This may be an unintended side effect of the Knack conversion.

Currently we are working around this, but we should develop a comprehensive solution and update these cases.

issue_title: Use standalone result library instead
issue_body:
As said in #477, the Result objects got split out into a separate library (https://github.com/python-trio/outcome). There's no point duplicating all the code in Trio - better to depend on it directly.

Todo:  
 - [ ] Gut result code inside of Trio  
 - [ ] Use the results from `outcome` instead.

Maybe make the current objects in Trio simply redirect to the outcome objects until the next version, deprecating them in 0.5 (with a redirect) and removing in 0.6.

issue_title: Support **kwargs in capture/acapture?
issue_body: I left this out of the original version of this code because in general in trio this is a complicated issue (https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/470), and I figured it was better to be consistent with other trio functions like `trio.run`, where we need to reserve kwargs for controlling the parent function. But now that it's a separate library, that doesn't really apply. And `capture` and `acapture` are obviously complete as they stand; there's no way we'll ever want to add any other arguments to them.

issue_title: Implement long-term solution for cross-interpreter requirement handling the in pex resolver
issue_body:
When a setup.py file specifies `install_requires` requirements with interpreter-specific constraints, the pex resolver cannot parse and handle these constraints to produce a cross-interpreter pex. See https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema/blob/master/setup.py#L43 for an example of such a constraint. 

Interpreter constraints like this are typically for stdlib backport libraries and the like that only make sense to include in a universal dist, so this problem is not as significant when the pex is targeting a specific interpreter version. In the targeted case, the resolver only needs to ignore requirements that are pinned to non-targeted interpreter versions. The short term workaround for the targeted-interpreter case is to allow the caller of `pex.resolver#resolve` to supply a blacklist dict that maps package name string -> interpreter constraint string, allowing the resolver to effectively skip them as implemented in (ADD PR). 

The scope of this ticket is to remove the blacklist implementation and replace it with smarter handling for the "pex-targeting-X-interpreter-version" case, specifically where all requirements are compared against interpreter constraints for a pex. Then at run time, the pex environment selectively activates packaged dependencies in accordance with a given pex's interpreter constraints. If no constraints are specified and the pex is intended to run with Python 2 or 3, then there should be support for determining this at runtime and handling env activation accordingly as well. 

This approach would solve the cross-interpreter case. This is a similar problem to the one surfaced in #455, however that issue deals with cross-platform specifications, but this is effectively the same concept.

issue_title: Pinger task in the artifact cache is not configurable, ignores configured caches silently
issue_body:
I am diagnosing some artifact cache issues. I noticed that the pinger task in cache_setup.py has no configuration options.  

```
    self._pinger = pinger or Pinger(timeout=0.5, tries=2)
```

There seem to be no visible diagnostics (without turning on debugging) if the pinger task fails and pants runs as if no artifact caches are visible.

issue_title: ProductGraph/BuildGraph facade
issue_body: This issue deals with adding an alternate BuildGraph implementation that consumes a completed ProductGraph. This will allow us to execute BUILD file parsing with the new engine.

issue_title: Port isolated process implementation to rust
issue_body:
Isolated processes currently work for the purposes of testing, but there are lots of abstraction leaks: in particular, a `_Snapshots` snapshots object is exposed which the python code uses to reverse engineer the location of snapshots.

Instead, process execution should be ported to rust (maybe using [duct](https://docs.rs/duct/0.8.1/duct/)?) to remove that leak.

issue_title: Follow redirects for HEAD in RESTfulArtifactCache
issue_body: Currently we take the requests library default which is to follow redirects for all but HEAD. If a cache server want's to send a HEAD redirect there is no reason we should not follow only for this particular method.

issue_body:

Also, I'm not sure what this flag is supposed to be doing. I

```
  --[no-]test-junit-per-test-timer
  --[no-]per-test-timer   Show progress and timer for each test. (default: None)

The timer is not for one method being tested but for the entire test run.     was really hoping to see a timer per test method and immediate feedback if the test fails rather than waiting for the end.  Is that what the intent of this flag is?  If so then there is more broken that just displaying 'null'.

issue_title: grpc introduces dependency on golang and cmake
issue_body:
394b1e7037886b0d9f579a0cc73f68b1c3a73d6a introduced a dependency on `cmake` and `golang`. They seem to be present in our `xcode6.4`  travis image, but they are less likely to be available on pants developer laptops.

A few options:
* formally introduce these dependencies on https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/README.md#requirements
* pre-build https://github.com/illicitonion/grpc-rs/blob/2acabdbff71e77a14b95b0b30c6d7de516355df7/grpc-sys/build.rs somehow
* check in the generated code, and have a separate set of instructions for updating it

cc @illicitonion , @kwlzn , @jsirois

issue_title: Use "Embedded in another view" with helpers and/or with "Implicit optional callback"
issue_body:
"Embedded in another view" + "Implicit optional callback":
Views.py
```python
class DataTableView(DatatableView):
    model = Agent 
    datatable_options = {
        'columns': [
            'firstname',
            'lastname',
            ("Certification", 'agent__certifications', 'get_agent_certifications'),
        ]
        }

    def get_agent_certifications(self, instance, *args, **kwargs):
        return ", ".join([Certification.long_name for Certification in instance.certifications.all()])


class SelectionAgentSearchView(LoginRequiredMixin, FormView):
    form_class = SearchForm
    template_name = 'client/datatable.html'
    success_url = 'client/datatable.html'


    def get_context_data(self, **kwargs):
        context = super(SelectionAgentSearchView, self).get_context_data(**kwargs)
        datatable_view = DataTableView()
        # Init Datatables parameters
        model = datatable_view.model
        options = datatable_view.get_datatable_options()
        # Retrieve the selection ID from the url to join with agents
        selectionid = self.kwargs['selectionid']
        datatable = get_datatable_structure(reverse('clients:agents_list'), options, model=model)
        context['datatable'] = datatable
        return context


def agents_list(request):
    if request.method == 'GET':
        agents = Agent.objects.filter().values_list(
            'firstname',
            'lastname',
            '', # Here should be the datas returned by implicit callback
    agents = {"data": list(agents)}
    return JsonResponse(agents, safe=False)
```
Of course, there is no datas in "Certification" column.
I think the way of doings things is putting the implicit callback ```def get_agent_certifications(self, instance, *args, **kwargs):
        return ", ".join([Certification.long_name for Certification in instance.certifications.all()])``` if ```agents_list``` function. Can you confirm or infirm please ?

I think I'll have the same issue with helpers ?

issue_title: Support lists/tuples as context.execute() parameters
issue_body: PEP 249 defines that `context.execute(statement, parameters)` should support parameters as sequences or mappings. If you pass, for instance a list, it fails with `AttributeError`

issue_title: Build/install broken without cython
issue_body:
## Long story short

Latest master cannot be installed without cython.
```
clang: error: no such file or directory: 'aiohttp/_websocket.c'
```

## Expected behaviour

```
pip install git+http://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp
```
Should install aiohttp even without cython. It worked at least in previous versions.

## Actual behaviour

Build failed:
```
    running build_ext
    building 'aiohttp._websocket' extension
    creating build/temp.macosx-10.13-x86_64-3.7
    creating build/temp.macosx-10.13-x86_64-3.7/aiohttp
    gcc -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -Wunreachable-code -fno-common -dynamic -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -I/Users/tosha/.pythonz/pythons/CPython-3.7.0/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.7/include/python3.7m -c aiohttp/_websocket.c -o build/temp.macosx-10.13-x86_64-3.7/aiohttp/_websocket.o
    clang: error: no such file or directory: 'aiohttp/_websocket.c'
    clang: error: no input files
    error: command 'gcc' failed with exit status 1
```

## Steps to reproduce

Run
```
pip install git+http://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp
```
in fresh empty venv.

## Your environment

macOS 10.13.6/Python 3.7/empty vevn
I am sure that the problem is OS/Python version independent.

issue_title: How do i upload huge files with aiobotocore.
Pagination is broken
issue_body:
## Issue

Commit 42dbd833187ff396e4b0e443eaa5fd0d20b84b66 appears to have broken pagination. The reproducer below works fine using aiobotocore 0.6.0 (with aiohttp 3.0.9), but breaks with aiobotocore 0.7.0 (with aiohttp 3.1.3).

```python
from pprint import pprint

import aiobotocore


async def main():
    session = aiobotocore.get_session()

    async with session.create_client('s3') as s3:
        paginator = s3.get_paginator('list_objects')
        async for page in paginator.paginate(Bucket='some-bucket'):
            for item in page.get('Contents', []):
                pprint(item)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

And the resulting traceback:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 50, in <module>
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.5/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py", line 468, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "test.py", line 42, in main
    for item in page.get('Contents', []):
AttributeError: 'coroutine' object has no attribute 'get'
sys:1: RuntimeWarning: coroutine 'AioPageIterator.next_page' was never awaited
```

## Root cause

The problem is in `AioPageIterator`'s `__anext__`:
https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore/blob/42fc4d9765d84b8d16168b750296859b85ac698e/aiobotocore/paginate.py#L93-L97

Rather than awaiting `self.next_page()`, the coroutine is being returned directly. This is definitely wrong; changing the last line to `return await self.next_page()` fixes the issue (I'll submit a PR).

What I don't understand is how it was working before, and why simply switching from using the `@asyncio.coroutine` decorator to native `async def` functions exposed it.

issue_title: If you disable backtracking, TP.py and TP10X2.py don't compute predictive cells
issue_body:
Create a TP with parameter:

~~~python
maxInfBacktrack=0
~~~

Run the TP.

RESULT:
To outside observers, this TP will always burst every active column, and it will never have a predictive cell.

Internally, the "learning" state ("lrnActiveState" and "lrnPredictedState") is right, but the "inference" state is wrong ("infActiveState" and "infPredictedState"). It never computes predictive cells in the inference state.

The bug: It never calls `inferPhase2` in this case. Here's the faulty code:

**TP.py:**

https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/bf38147eb2215b5243d123e411133a8e0ff6d3c9/src/nupic/research/TP.py#L1813

https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/bf38147eb2215b5243d123e411133a8e0ff6d3c9/src/nupic/research/TP.py#L1522

**TP10X2.py:**

https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/b9b1f224f6a6b6bde09a4364fd4d72549ec3f6af/src/nupic/algorithms/Cells4.cpp#L1066

https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/b9b1f224f6a6b6bde09a4364fd4d72549ec3f6af/src/nupic/algorithms/Cells4.cpp#L297

issue_title: Manual download of nupic.bindings no longer needed
issue_body: We previously had logic for building against unreleased nupic.bindings versions (via `.nupic_modules`) and after that still required manually fetching nupic.bindings from S3 for Linux. These cases are no longer necessary so we should remove code that manually fetches nupic.bindings or references to the old S3 location for intermediary builds.

issue_title: Drop the declare_namespace call from bindings/py/nupic/bindings/__init__.py
issue_body:
This is an easy beginner task.

"nupic" is the only namespace package being intentionally declared (see `namespace_packages` in `bindings/py/setup.py`).

The `declare_namespace` call in bindings/py/nupic/bindings/__init__.py appears to be declaring an additional namespace package "nupic.bindings", but as far as I know this is unintentional, and the lack of "nupic.bindings" in `namespace_packages` in `bindings/py/setup.py` likely corroborates this.

So, just delete the `declare_namespace` call from `bindings/py/nupic/bindings/__init__.py`.

issue_title: Travis output archive includes file absolute paths (it shouldn't)
timeout for stack deletion should not be fixed to 10min
issue_body: Cfn-Sphere uses a fixed timeout of 10min for stack deletion. We should use the timeout value which is already used for stack creation.

issue_body:
I'm completely new to `folium` and `leaflet` as of today, so maybe this is a silly question. I am plotting a `LineString` (though `Polygon` has the same behavior) on map and I noticed that I get visual artifacts when I scroll the map such that the perimeter is off the viewable area.

From googling around, I *think* it sounds and looks pretty much like [this issue](https://github.com/Leaflet/Leaflet/issues/2814).

Here's a simple version to illustrate:
```
import folium

lat, lon = (44, -93)
m = folium.Map(location=(lat, lon),

               style_function=lambda x: {'color': '#999999',

issue_title: `ensure_unicode_string()` throws an exception when argument is an Exception with non-ASCII bytes in the message
issue_body:
If an object (like an Exception) is passed into ensure_unicode_string and the exception has non-ASCII bytes in the message, it will raise a UnicodeError (which will be caught) and then makes the false assumption that the argument must be a string (and therefore have a .decode() method) resulting in an AttributeError being raised.

This can happen whenever a test raises an exception that has a message containing non-ASCII bytes. When that happens, the exception gets passed to `ensure_unicode_string()` here https://github.com/box/flaky/blob/master/flaky/_flaky_plugin.py#L43 and raises an exception.

Test case:
```python
def test_ensure_unicode_string_handles_nonascii_exception_message(self):
        ex = Exception(u'\u2013'.encode('utf-8'))

        string = ensure_unicode_string(ex)
        self.assertEqual(string, u'\u2013')
```

issue_title: Revamp handling of fields without declared field
issue_body:
This is a follow-up from https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/783#issuecomment-389027969.

Currently, there's a fair amount of complexity in updating `self.fields` on `Schema`.

The current logic is quite odd and has a number of edge cases. It looks a little bit like:

1. When dumping
    1. Check if the object type is in `_types_seen`
    2. If it's not in `_types_seen`, infer the field types for anything in e.g. `Schema.Meta.fields` but without a declared field from the types on the object to be dumped
    3. If the object is not of a mapping type, add its type to `_types_seen`
        1. Note that this includes `list`, which is what we get whenever dumping with `many=True`
2. When loading
    1. Just use whatever is in `self.fields` from the last run
3. For `fields.Nested`
    1. Only calculate `fields` once for every instance of `fields.Nested`

Assuming my understanding is correct, this leads to a number of weird things happening.

For example, if I try to dump an object of type `Foo`, then try to dump a `dict`, then try to dump another object of type `Foo`, the `fields` used will be the ones corresponding to the `dict`.

Likewise, if I keep trying to dump additional `dict`s, the fields well get recalculated every time. However, this won't happen if I try to repeatedly dump `list`s with `many=True`.

But when using a `fields.Nested`, the field inference will only happen once.

Additionally, this is all potentially non-thread-safe.

-----

This is all quite complicated. My proposal for a simpler option is to add a new `fields.Default` that has the relevant logic, and instead only infer the fields once at schema instantiation time.

issue_title: MySQL - #1071 - Specified key was too long; max key length is 767 bytes
issue_body:
Hi, 

I wanted to migrate from sqlite to mysql and had following problem. I tracked it down and found following:

This is my table definition:
db.define_table('testtable',
                Field('teststring','string',unique=True)
                )

which translates to following mysql-code:
CREATE TABLE testtable(
    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT NOT NULL,
    teststring VARCHAR(512) UNIQUE,
    PRIMARY KEY (id)
) ENGINE=InnoDB CHARACTER SET utf8;

and following error message:
#1071 - Specified key was too long; max key length is 767 bytes 
 
It seems that the unique=True causes that kind of problem. If I omit the unique=True, it works:
db.define_table('testtable2',
                Field('teststring','string')
                )
CREATE TABLE testtable2(
    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT NOT NULL,
    teststring VARCHAR(512),
    PRIMARY KEY (id)
) ENGINE=InnoDB CHARACTER SET utf8;

The varchar(512) is too long, if I reduce it to a varchar(255) it works (>= 256 does not work).

I am using web2py 2.10.4 and mysql 5.5.39 (MySQL Community Server (GPL)) on RHEL6. Probably a pretty easy fix would be to translate a dal string into a varchar(255), but I blame Mysql here ...

issue_title: Patsy loses DatetimeIndex freq information even if no NA values
issue_body:
(This is probably better described as a pandas bug, see https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/21282, but maybe patsy wants to patch this too?)

**Reproducible example**:

(proceeding from the above code)

```python
import pandas as pd
import patsy

index = pd.DatetimeIndex(start='1990', end='1994', freq='AS')
data = pd.Series([0, 1, 2, 3, 4], name='y', index=index)
print(data.index)

lhs, rhs = patsy.dmatrices('y ~ 1', data={'y':data}, return_type='dataframe')
print(lhs.index)
```

The first print statement yields:

```
DatetimeIndex(['1990-01-01', '1991-01-01', '1992-01-01', '1993-01-01',
               '1994-01-01'],
              dtype='datetime64[ns]', freq='AS-JAN')
```

Whereas the second yields:

```
DatetimeIndex(['1990-01-01', '1991-01-01', '1992-01-01', '1993-01-01',
               '1994-01-01'],
              dtype='datetime64[ns]', freq=None)
```

This is a consequence of https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/21282 as it affects the following function in `patsy/missing.py`:

```python
def _handle_NA_drop(self, values, is_NAs, origins):
        total_mask = np.zeros(is_NAs[0].shape[0], dtype=bool)
        for is_NA in is_NAs:
            total_mask |= is_NA
        good_mask = ~total_mask
        # "..." to handle 1- versus 2-dim indexing
        return [v[good_mask, ...] for v in values]
```

when `v` is the `DatetimeIndex`, the ellipses cause the index to lose frequency information.

issue_title: doctests not included?
datetime migrator (archetypes->dexterity) off for some timezones in newer pytz versions
issue_body:
I am not sure if this is an issue with pytz or with this package, but it's probably something this package would want to be aware of since there is already "datetime_fixer" code. The issue I ran into was with dates that are in US/Eastern (probably all US/*) in versions of pytz 2015.7 or newer. It is not an issue on pytz 2013b (I've only tested 2013b, 2015.7, and 2017.2).

Assuming this value for an event startDate, DateTime('2017/02/23 19:00:00 US/Eastern').

    >>> import pytz
    >>> pytz.timezone('US/Eastern')
    <DstTzInfo 'US/Eastern' LMT-1 day, 19:04:00 STD>

I have no idea where it is getting that 4 minute discrepancy, but it results in the converted datetime object being off by 4 minutes too.

The other issue is that it is looking for a 'timezone' field in the schema when really the timezone is part of the DateTime object. Perhaps it would make more sense to do something like this?

    old_timezone = old_value.timezone()
    new_value = datetime_fixer(old_value.asdatetime().replace(tzinfo=None), old_timezone)

That is not a complete solution, in part because pytz would expect 'Etc/GMT-4' and not 'GMT-4'.

issue_title: Add an infinite feed implementation
issue_body:
The original feed implementation's intent seemed to be that of an infinite feed where if a `last_seq` was encountered as part of a continuous feed then another http request would be initiaited to the feed endpoint using the last_seq value as the "since" value thus creating an infinite feed iterator.

Since the feed was rewritten (as part of #45) to be more in line with how the `_changes` and `_db_updates` feed endpoints and their options function, this infinite feed functionality has been somewhat lost.

This issue tracks the re-addition of the infinite feed functionality back into this library.

Consider an InfiniteFeed class??

issue_title: Versioning typo

The README and docs/index.rst contains a typo.

They both contains the following sentence:

"For **Elasticsearch 2.0** and later, use the major version 1 (``1.x.y``) of the library."

Major version of the library for Elasticsearch 2.0 should be 2, not 1.

issue_title: Initial recovery of standby never succeeds when using --terse: FATAL:  the database system is starting up
issue_body:
Initial recovery of standby fails when using --terse. To reproduce this annoying bug, simply follow the "Fetching a backup" instructions at https://gist.github.com/elithrar/8682235, except add the `--terse` option to the `restore_command` in `recovery.conf`.

You will find that postgresql won't complete its startup procedure, and your logs will fill up an endless loop of this message:

```
< 2016-04-27 07:58:19.302 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:20.304 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:21.306 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:22.308 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:23.310 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:24.319 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:25.321 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:26.323 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:27.325 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:28.326 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
< 2016-04-27 07:58:29.337 UTC >FATAL:  the database system is starting up
```

The workaround is to simply remove the `--terse` option, restart postgresql, then put the `--terse` option back in. This would be fine if we were manually managing a small number of standbys, but trying to do this via puppet at scale is a nightmare.

issue_title: StaticStyle.image: twox=True generates a URL that the MapBox API barfs on
issue_body:
```
import mapbox
import os

style = mapbox.StaticStyle(access_token=os.environ['MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN'])
response = style.image(
    username='tomjakubowskiob',
    style_id='cj383j11j00052roea199zqcv',
    zoom=2.0, lon=0.00, lat=0.00,
    pitch=0.0, bearing=0.0,
    width=640,
    height=640,
    twox=True)  # setting this to False also results in success, but w/ no @2x rendering
print response.status_code
print response.request.url
```

On my machine, this prints `404` and then something like `https://api.mapbox.com/styles/v1/tomjakubowskiob/cj383j11j00052roea199zqcv/static/0.0,0.0,2.0,0.0,0.0/640x640%402x?access_token=NOT_TELLING_YOU`

When I run that same URL through curl (being careful to quote the URL at the shell), I also see a 404.

```
$ curl -vo out.png 'https://api.mapbox.com/styles/v1/tomjakubowskiob/cj383j11j00052roea199zqcv/static/0.0,0.0,2.0,0.0,0.0/640x640%402x?access_token=NOT_TELLING_YOU'
...
< HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
...
```

However, if "unescape" by hand the `%40` to a `@` character in the curl invocation, I finally obtain a successful map image.

```

...
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
...
```

issue_title: Improved wash_urlargd()
issue_body:
Originally by *[pglauner](https://github.com/pglauner)* on 2011-10-11

Idea: There is an argument xyz:
```
argd = wash_urlargd(form, {'xyz': (int, DEFAULT)})
```
Suggest that xyz must be >= 0. Negative values might be caught by
```
if argd['xyz'] < 0:
   do something
```
or are not caught at all, which might result in an exception or even worse.


wash_urlargd() should be extended to handle this.

Alternatively, a complete new function could be created to handle a lot of other cool things like accepting a list of permitted values &c.

issue_title: LESS_RUN_IN_DEBUG has effect only on first load
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:

1. Setup an Invenio pu instance as described by `INSTALL.rst`, including the demosite.
2. Have the following additional settings in `invenio.cfg`:

    (invenio2)~/.virtualenvs/invenio2/var/invenio.base-instance$ cat invenio.cfg
    [...]
    COLLECT_STORAGE = u'flask.ext.collect.storage.link'
    DEBUG = True
    LESS_RUN_IN_DEBUG = False
    ASSETS_DEBUG = True
    DEBUG_TB_INTERCEPT_REDIRECTS = False
    TESTING = True

3. Run `inveniomanage runserver` and see that `less.js` is running:

issue_labels: [{'id': 96304291, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Branch:%20maint-1.1', 'name': 'Branch: maint-1.1', 'color': 'd4c5f9', 'default': False}, {'id': 96240745, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Component:%20BibCirculation', 'name': 'Component: BibCirculation', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}, {'id': 414883170, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Size:%20beginner-friendly', 'name': 'Size: beginner-friendly', 'color': '8646c6', 'default': False}, {'id': 96237777, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Type:%20bug', 'name': 'Type: bug', 'color': 'ff2a00', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
For generation of the Holdings-tab invenio checks if all items are set to status `missing`. If all items are missing the display changes to a link for the ILL subsystem to enable patrons to order items. As long as only one item is associated with a record, this works fine. However, if more than one item is associated with a given record the ILL-display is wrongly triggered. The error stems from `get_copies_status()` in `bibcirculation_dblayer`. Though the name suggests that it returns the status of all copies it effectifely returns `res[0]`, ie. the status of exactly one copy.

This function is only used `all_copies_are_missing()` and `has_copies()` within `bibcirculation_utils`.

issue_title: Fedora 20: "cgcloud create" gets confused by cloud-init restarting ssh
Feature request: a special "<text>" value for the hooks.yaml "files" field
issue_body:
Hi,

I had to implement this kind of "binary-files-filtering-out" today:
https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/pre_commit_hooks/utils.py#L10

So I made a quick overview of the existing checkers, and I found only 2 that are targeting **any** text file: [end-of-file-fixer](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/hooks.yaml#L76) and [trailing-whitespaces](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/hooks.yaml#L106). Both use a long list of known text files suffixes.

If ever you think this use case could come in handy, I'd suggest to provide a magic value like `<any text file>` to the `files` parameter in _hooks.yaml_, so that hooks developers don't have to re-implement this filtering each time.

You can freely re-use the code I mentioned above if it comes handy to you.

Also, sorry for the 3-issues-opened-in-a-row :) I got really excited by this tool and ideas for improvements just flowed in ! But they are really just humble features suggestions.

Cheers

issue_title: coala
issue_body:
Hi,

we are currently writing a language independent static code analysis framework named coala - coala-analyzer.org . As part of that goal we offer a unified user interface to lots of common linters and provide stuff like json output and so on for them.

I think pre-commit could profit from coala in that you would not have to maintain that many tool integrations anymore since coala could act as a proxy here (we do that anyways). On long term that is less work for you and for your users because they can configure everything in one config file, don't need to learn multiple tools.

Is there any interest in making a coala integration happen?

issue_title: Consider adding "pipenv outdated" as an alias for "pipenv update --outdated"
issue_body: Pipenv doesn't support `outdated` as a separate subcommand, making the functionality sort of hard to reach. [Yarn](https://yarnpkg.com/lang/en/docs/cli/outdated/), [npm](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/outdated), [Composer](https://getcomposer.org/doc/03-cli.md#outdated), [RubyGems](https://guides.rubygems.org/command-reference/#gem-outdated), [Bundler](https://bundler.io/v1.16/bundle_outdated.html) and Cargo (via a [third party plugin](https://github.com/kbknapp/cargo-outdated)) all support `$package_manager outdated` directly. It would be nice for Pipenv to be consistent here, as many people working on full stack projects probably have muscle memory for it (I know I do :) ).

issue_title: (Module) file permissions not set correctly upon install
issue_body:
When installing a python package system-wide with setuptools, using `zip_safe=False`, on Linux, the file permissions are not updated to make them world-readable. This effectively breaks the installation.

This is an example source tree:

```
.
├── [-rw-r-----]  setup.py
└── [drwxr-x---]  toyproject
    ├── [-rw-r-----]  ball.py
    └── [-rw-r-----]  __init__.py
```

Note that in the source tree, I do not allow other users to see my files. This is intentional.

My `setup.py` contains:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
#from distutils.core import setup
from setuptools import setup
setup(
    name="toyproject",
    package_dir={'toyproject': 'toyproject'},
    packages=['toyproject'],
    zip_safe=False,
)
```

The file `__init__.py` is empty and `ball.py` contains:

```python
from __future__ import print_function
print("ball")
```

After running `sudo python setup.py install` and changing to a different directory, I cannot import `toyproject.ball`:

```
Python 2.7.14 (default, Feb 17 2018, 10:42:17) 
[GCC 7.3.1 20180130 (Red Hat 7.3.1-2)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import toyproject.ball
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named ball
>>> 
```

The reason is that the permissions and ownerships of the installed files make it impossible to import the `ball` module:

```
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/toyproject-0.0.0-py2.7.egg
├── [drwxr-xr-x root     root    ]  EGG-INFO
│   ├── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  dependency_links.txt
│   ├── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  not-zip-safe
│   ├── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  PKG-INFO
│   ├── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  SOURCES.txt
│   └── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  top_level.txt
└── [drwxr-xr-x root     root    ]  toyproject
    ├── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  ball.py
    ├── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  ball.pyc
    ├── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  __init__.py
    └── [-rw-r----- root     root    ]  __init__.pyc
```

I would have expected the following permissions:

```
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/toyproject-0.0.0-py2.7.egg
├── [drwxr-xr-x root     root    ]  EGG-INFO
│   ├── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  dependency_links.txt
│   ├── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  not-zip-safe
│   ├── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  PKG-INFO
│   ├── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  SOURCES.txt
│   └── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  top_level.txt
└── [drwxr-xr-x root     root    ]  toyproject
    ├── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  ball.py
    ├── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  ball.pyc
    ├── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  __init__.py
    └── [-rw-r--r-- root     root    ]  __init__.pyc
```

Setting these permissions manually fixes the problem, but it is obviously inconvenient that setuptools does not take care of this.

There are other ways to solve this, but each of them has its own set of limitations:

- Use distutils. (not ok because I need other setuptools features)
- Use ` zip_safe=True`. (not ok in several of my projects, e.g. because then nosetests cannot discover unit tests.)

Anyway, my reasons for using `zip_safe=False` are not so relevant.

issue_title: Replace easy_install with pip install
issue_body:
There are many open issues with Setuptools that stem from the differences between easy_install and pip install. Now that #250 is complete and there exists a mechanism by which pip install can largely satisfy the use cases currently filled by easy_install. Perhaps at some point I will link to or enumerate the issues that would be addressed by such a replacement but not right now.

In the replacement, I imagine the following:

Invocations of `easy_install` whether from a command-line entry point or an implicit invocation as part of a `setup.py install` will instead shell out to pip to perform the equivalent operation. Some options may become unavailable while others will be translated to their pip equivalent.

One big open question is whether installs should still be done as eggs (using the deprecated `pip install --egg`) or if it should rely on pip's use of wheel. I'm leaning toward the latter.

There are probably other open issues that I haven't yet considered.

issue_title: Visualizing file content
issue_body:
We currently store the content of all files created and modified during a trial. It would be nice to allow users to click in one function and see all created and modified files. Moreover, in the case of the modified files, we could run a diff against its previous version and show the change itself.

Basically, the idea is similar to what SourceTree does: different panels, one with the list of files and the other with the diff content of the file, but instead of having commits we would have functions to navigate.

issue_title: FluentRecordFormatter not compatible with Python 3.2+ Formatter
issue_body:
From Python 3.2 `logging.Formatter` `__init__` method has extra parameter `style`:
```python 
def __init__(self, fmt=None, datefmt=None, style='%'):
```
but `fluent.handler.FluentRecordFormatter` does not have it:
```python
def __init__(self, fmt=None, datefmt=None):
```
Problem appears when reading logging configuration from a ConfigParser-format file using
`logging.fileConfig` (e.g. loading logging configuration from Pyramid ini file). 
When `logging.config._create_formatters` runs `f = c(fs, dfs, stl)` following exception is raised:
`TypeError: __init__() takes from 1 to 3 positional arguments but 4 were given`

I suggest to add `style` parameter to `fluent.handler.FluentRecordFormatter` `__init__` definition.

```python

metadata.create_all()

issue_title: reduce word wrap violence in discussions
Enable setting PYTHONHOME to support applications that distribute Python
issue_body:
There's been a request to set PYTHONHOME for an application that distributes Python with the application.  The Python C-API also supports this: 

- https://docs.python.org/2/c-api/init.html#c.Py_SetPythonHome
- https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/init.html#c.Py_SetPythonHome

There's an open question of the route to take to set it: if we should use the C-API call `Py_SetPythonHome(arg)` or use Java's `System.loadLibrary("python")` or `System.load(filename)`.

See https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/jep-project/9TOYn3ElbFw for more details.

issue_title: Add a Hook To Customize the TransactionManager instace
issue_body:
Currently ``pyramid_tm`` more or less requires using the same transaction manager for every request, which more or less means that it *must* be the thread local transaction manager if the application needs to be thread safe. It would be great if a ``manager_hook` could be added which was a callable which was passed the ``request`` and is expected to return the transaction manager for this particular request. This would enable applications to use explicit transaction managers instead of relying on a thread local one.

The patch itself should be fairly simply, here's a small diff that does what I'd like (lacking tests and documentation though):
```diff
diff --git a/pyramid_tm/__init__.py b/pyramid_tm/__init__.py
index 937cecc..34f1409 100644
--- a/pyramid_tm/__init__.py
+++ b/pyramid_tm/__init__.py
@@ -35,9 +35,11 @@ def tm_tween_factory(handler, registry, transaction=transaction):
     old_commit_veto = registry.settings.get('pyramid_tm.commit_veto', None)
     commit_veto = registry.settings.get('tm.commit_veto', old_commit_veto)
     activate = registry.settings.get('tm.activate_hook')
+    manager_hook = registry.settings.get('tm.manager_hook')
     attempts = int(registry.settings.get('tm.attempts', 1))
     commit_veto = resolver.maybe_resolve(commit_veto) if commit_veto else None
     activate = resolver.maybe_resolve(activate) if activate else None
+    manager_hook = resolver.maybe_resolve(manager_hook) if manager_hook else None
     assert attempts > 0
 
     def tm_tween(request):
@@ -49,7 +51,11 @@ def tm_tween_factory(handler, registry, transaction=transaction):
             if not activate(request):
                 return handler(request)
 
-        manager = transaction.manager
+        if manager_hook is not None:
+            manager = manager_hook(request)
+        else:
+            manager = transaction.manager
+
         number = attempts
         if hasattr(request, 'unauthenticated_userid'):
             userid = request.unauthenticated_userid

```

issue_title: Sending stats admin URL doesn't work with Django > 1.6
issue_body:

    `from django.conf.urls.defaults import patterns, url`

With newer versions of Django (after 1.6), this gives an `ImportError: No module named defaults`

The correct line to use is:  `from django.conf.urls import patterns, url`

issue_title: Roundtrip fails: Tile -> pixel -> point -> meter -> Tile
issue_body:
As far as I can see, the following assertion mustn't fail.

```python
def test_roundtrip():
    zoom_level = 18
    t = Tile.from_tms(139423, 171197, zoom_level)
    x, y = t.pixel
    p = Point.from_pixel(x, y, zoom=zoom_level)
    mx, my = p.meters
    t2 = Tile.for_meters(mx, my, zoom_level)
    assert t.quad_tree == t2.quad_tree
```

Note, that ```pixel``` is defined in ```Tile``` as:

```python
@property
def pixel(self):
    google_x, google_y = self.google
    pixel_x_west, pixel_y_north = google_x * TILE_SIZE, google_y * TILE_SIZE
    pixel_x_east, pixel_y_south = (google_x + 1) * TILE_SIZE, (google_y + 1) * TILE_SIZE
    return pixel_x_west, pixel_x_east
```

issue_title: Error in TPA for VkD
issue_body:
An error coming from TPA appears when I use VkD-CMA for certain functions of the [coco](https://github.com/numbbo/coco) `bbob-largescale` suite. In particular, the error comes from the lines 464 and 469 [here](https://github.com/CMA-ES/pycma/blob/master/cma/restricted_gaussian_sampler.py) - the two symmetric points around the mean along the mean shift are not identified at the set of sampled solutions. I think it's a numerical issue because of the fact that the Mahalanobis norm obtains large values of order `10^13` and the norm of `dy` becomes very small - see lines 410 and 411 [here](https://github.com/CMA-ES/pycma/blob/master/cma/restricted_gaussian_sampler.py). 

An example for the Sharp Ridge function in dimension 20: 

```
import cma
import numpy as np
import cocoex
from cma import restricted_gaussian_sampler as rgs
N = 20                                                    
k = int(np.log(N))
bbob = cocoex.Suite('bbob-largescale', '', '')
f = bbob.get_problem_by_function_dimension_instance(13, N, 1)  
x0 = 10*(np.random.rand(N)-0.5)
sigma0 = 0.02*10
f._best_parameter('print')
x_opt = np.loadtxt('._bbob_problem_best_parameter.txt')
f_0 = lambda x: f(x_opt + x)
cma.fmin(f_0, x0, sigma0, options = rgs.GaussVkDSampler.extend_cma_options({
    'CMA_sampler_options' : {'kadapt' : True, 'k_init' : k}}))
cma.plot()
```

VkD - CMA : 
![sharpridgevkd](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32420824/39189666-0ec86582-47d3-11e8-88a3-2dc98013b273.png)

CMA:
![sharpridgecma](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32420824/39189703-2d9309a4-47d3-11e8-8510-7d5eb12619e1.png)

Except the Sharp Ridge function, it appears in other cases too, for example: in dimension 20 for the Weierstrass, Schaffer and Katsuuras functions (and for more functions in larger dimensions).

issue_title: C4 Conflicts
issue_body:
Our README states that we're using C4, but we're not really:

* No longer doing the Problem -- Solution format on commits and PRs
* Commits are now expected to use conventional changelog
* We rely on BSD instead of MPL

We also should consider updating our [version of C4 to spec 42](https://rfc.zeromq.org/spec:42/C4) from zeromq.

This is currently a bit awkward because the things we're saying and the things were doing don't match right now.

I personally prefer specific virtues under C4, which aim to accept people (by accepting their patches) pretty readily (optimistic merging), even if their commit messages don't match directly. I'd prefer to merge and fix after -- while also leading the contributor to the way to fix it.

issue_title: Do not wipe-out the data directory if the replica creation method returned 0, but the start failed
issue_body:
We had an issue in the field:
- the replica has been initialized successfully from a multi-GB backup
- the replica failed to start, because max_connections there were set to the lower master than on the master
- the replica is wiped out and the backup is started anew.

Should we change the logic to reinitialize the replica only if the replica method fails, and do nothing if the actual start was unsuccessful?

issue_title: Silencing ImportErrors raised in modules referenced by operationId
issue_body:
To reproduce simply create an incorrect import that will raise an ImportError in a module that is referenced by operationId of an endpoint specified in a swagger API.


```
project/swagger/api.yaml
---------------------------------
...
paths:
    /scms:
         get:
             operationId:
                  api.scms.test
...



project/src/api/scms.py
---------------------------------
#broken import
import something_broken

def test():
    return {'test': 'ok'}, 200

```

When URL `/api/scms` is requested the module raises an `ImportError` and the test method is not reachable, but connexion proceeds and it says:
```{"status": 404, "type": "about:blank", "detail": "The requested URL was not found on the server.  If you entered the URL manually please check your spelling and try again.", "title": "Not Found"}```
without re-raising the exception so it is not clear what failed.
